column1: Mor developed a formal style for court portraits
column2: largely based on Titian
column3: that was extremely influential on court painters across Europe
column4: especially in the Iberian Peninsula
column5: where it created a tradition that led to Diego Velázquez. His works can include considerable psychological penetration
column6: especially in portraits of men
column7: but always gives the subject a grand and self-possessed air. Mor was born in Utrecht
column9: by some estimation between 1516 and 1520. Little is known about his early life
column10: except that his artistic education commenced under Jan van Scorel. His earliest known work is a portrait which is now in a collection in Stockholm
column11: dated 1538.  A group of Knights of St. John at Utrecht
column12: supposed to have been painted about 1541; and a picture of two pilgrims at the Gemäldegalerie
column14: dated 1544; and the portrait of an unknown woman
column15: in the Palais des Beaux-Arts de Lille
column16: were probably among his earliest works. Their authenticity has not been established. In 1547 Mor was received as a member of the Venerable Guild of St. Luke at Antwerp
column17: and shortly afterwards (about 1548) he attracted the attention of Cardinal Antoine Perrenot de Granvelle
column19: who became his steady patron. Of the portraits executed during the early period of his career as de Granvelle's protégé

column21: and one of the Duke of Alba
column22: which now belongs to the Hispanic Society of America. Between 1549 and 1550 Philip
column23: Prince of Asturias (1527–1598) traveled around the Netherlands to present himself as the future ruler. Mor painted his portrait in Brussels in 1549. He probably visited Italy (when exactly is not known)
column24: where he copied some works by Titian
column25: notably the Danaë. In the middle of 1550 Mor left for Lisbon with a commission from Mary of Hungary to portray the Portuguese branch of the family. Mor probably traveled via Valladolid
column26: where he painted the portraits of Maximilian II and his wife Maria of Austria
column27: their daughter Anna and the son of Philip II of Spain
column31: Prince Joăo Manuel and Philip II's future wife
column32: Princess Maria. Little more is known about Mor's stay in Portugal
column33: but he was definitely back in Brussels by November 1553. After the sudden death of the king of England
column37: Holy Roman Emperor now saw the possibility of an alliance between Spain and England. The engagement between Philip and his Portuguese princess was broken and negotiations started for a marriage with the successor to the English throne

column1: Mor was very productive after Philip's ascension to the throne
column2: and produced some of his most important portraits in this period

column9: whom Mor portrayed c.?1561. This portrait appears to have been lost. Also from this period dates the only known self-portrait of Mor
column12: now in the Prado (see image gallery below). It seems likely that Mor accompanied King Philip on his return to Spain in 1559. That Mor stayed at the Spanish court is confirmed by the letters which Philip regularly sent to Mor after he had left again in 1561. In his letters
column13: Philip requested Mor's return to court several times
column14: but the painter never complied with his repeated requests. Among the works which Mor supposedly painted in Spain are the Portrait of Juana of Austria and the Portrait of Don Carlos. A much-praised work from this period is the Portrait of Pejerón
column15: the fool of the Duke of Benavente and the Duke of Alba. There has been extensive speculation about the reason for Mor's departure from the Spanish court. According to Carel van Mander
column16: Mor became too confidential with the King and this aroused the suspicion of the Inquisition. He may also have been alarmed by the increasingly repressive Counter-Reformation tenor of the Spanish court.[4] Mor's pupil Alonso Sánchez Coello continued to work in his master's style
column17: and replaced him as the Spanish court painter. On his return to the Netherlands
column18: Mor probably traveled back and forth between Utrecht
column19: Antwerp and Brussels. In this period he was in regular contact with de Granvelle and also worked at the Dutch court
column20: where he portrayed Margaret of Parma. After his return
column21: Mor focused on the portrayal of citizens
column22: especially of merchants and their wives in Antwerp. In addition to portraits like these (which included the Portrait of Thomas Gresham)
column24: such as the goldsmith Steven van Herwijck (1564). These works are markedly different from the paintings Mor produced for the court
column25: showing off another side of his talent. When de Granvelle returned to France and the Netherlands showed increasing social and political unrest
column26: Mor experienced some financial hardship. His financial problems were partially solved when the Duke of Alba granted him commissions and favors. He is not known to have been in Utrecht after 24 July 1567 and from 1568 onwards Mor lived in Antwerp where
column28: he registered as a master with the Antwerp guild. At Antwerp he painted a Venus and Adonis for the new Stadhuis. It is possible that he visited England once again in 1568

column31: Anne of Austria spent some time in Antwerp
column32: where she was painted by Mor in 1570. Mor's portrait of Anna is his last-known court painting
column33: although he was still being referred to as Philip II's court painter in 1573.

column1: Little is known about Mor's life and career after 1570. It seems likely that he lost custom
column2: maybe as result of competition by painters such as Adriaen Thomasz Key
column3: Frans Pourbus the Elder (1545–1581) and Frans Floris (1519/20–1570). The last portrait attributed to Mor is the Portrait of Hubertus Goltzius
column4: dated 1576. Toward the end of his life
column5: Mor focused on history paintings of religious and mythological subjects
column6: but in this field of work he would never equal his earlier success as a portrait painter. He is believed to have been working on a Circumcision for the Cathedral of Antwerp when he died in 1576."

column6: with his wife Lena
